title: Informing Consumers about Smart Devices Act
short_title: Informing Consumers about Smart Devices Act
stage_label: Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. Reported by Senator Cruz without amendment. With written report No.
plain_summary: This law says that companies must tell customers before they buy smart devices if those devices have cameras or microphones inside them. Smart devices are things that connect to the internet like smart TVs, smart speakers, or smart home gadgets. The law does not apply to phones, tablets, or laptops because people already know those devices have cameras and microphones. The Federal Trade Commission will make sure companies follow this rule and will help companies understand what they need to do.
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 47.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Informing Consumers about Smart Devices Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Required Disclosure of a Camera or Recording Capability in Certain Internet-Connected Devices
sections.text: Every company that makes a covered device must tell customers clearly and obviously before they buy the device whether it has a camera or microphone built into it.

sections.title: Section 3 Enforcement by the Federal Trade Commission
sections.text: If a company breaks the rule in Section 2, it will be treated as an unfair or deceptive business practice under existing Federal Trade Commission rules. The Federal Trade Commission will enforce this law using the same powers and methods it uses for other consumer protection laws. Companies that break this law can face the same penalties as other violations of Federal Trade Commission rules. This law does not limit any other authority the Federal Trade Commission already has. Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the Federal Trade Commission must create guidance to help companies follow this law. The guidance will include best practices for making disclosures clear and easy to understand for all ages, including using pictures or symbols when helpful. A company can ask the Federal Trade Commission for specific guidance about how to meet the requirements for their particular device. Any guidance from the Federal Trade Commission does not give anyone special rights and does not force the commission to follow the guidance in every case. In enforcement actions, the Federal Trade Commission must point to a specific violation of this law, not just say a company failed to follow guidance.

sections.title: Section 4 Definition of Covered Device
sections.text: A covered device means a consumer product that can connect to the internet and has a camera or microphone as part of it. This does not include phones including mobile phones, laptops, tablets, or any device that a customer would reasonably expect to have a microphone or camera. It also does not include any device that is specifically sold as a camera, phone equipment, or microphone. Finally, it does not include certain telecommunications devices that are already regulated under other parts of federal communications law.

sections.title: Section 5 Effective Date
sections.text: This law will apply to all covered devices made after one hundred eighty days from when the Federal Trade Commission issues its guidance. It will not apply to covered devices that were made or sold before that date or otherwise entered the marketplace before that date.
